title: Dot Patterns
subtitle: Search + change + dot. The two main refactor idioms.
summary: The two best refactor patterns in Vim both pair search with the dot command. *cw…<Esc>n. is the classic; cgn. is the smoother modern version that lets you skip without thinking.

blocks.text: Two idioms make Vim feel like a refactoring tool. Both compose search with the dot command. They look almost identical, but the second one is much more pleasant to use.

Cursor on `foo`. {key:*} jumps to the next `foo` and stashes it as the search pattern. {key:c}{key:w} changes the word. Type the replacement. {key:Esc}. Now {key:n} jumps to the next match; {key:.} repeats the change. {key:n} {key:.} {key:n} {key:.} … walks you through, with the option to skip with {key:n} alone.

Same setup, but use {key:c}{key:g}{key:n} instead of {key:c}{key:w}. The {key:g}{key:n} motion means "the next search match." So {key:c}{key:g}{key:n} = change the next match. After the first one, you can just press {key:.} repeatedly — each {key:.} jumps *and* changes. Skip an occurrence by pressing {key:n} once before {key:.}.

blocks.title: Why cgn. Is Better
blocks.text:
{key:g}{key:n}'s genius is that it bakes the *jump* into the motion. So {key:c}{key:g}{key:n} doesn't need a separate {key:n} between repetitions — the motion finds the next match and selects it as part of the change.

Result: pure {key:.} {key:.} {key:.} repeats. Mental load goes from two-key cadence ({key:n}{key:.} {key:n}{key:.}) to one-key cadence ({key:.} {key:.}).

The analogous yank/delete forms exist: {key:y}{key:g}{key:n}, {key:d}{key:g}{key:n}. But change is the common one because refactor = change.

{key:g}{key:N} is the same idea but for the *previous* match, in case you want to walk backward.

These patterns are why {key:.} is called "the most important key in Vim." Combined with {key:*} and {key:c}{key:g}{key:n}, every grep-and-replace becomes a guided walk: you can preview each change, undo with {key:u}, skip with {key:n}, accept with {key:.}.

rows: Smooth: change next match, then pure {key:.} repeats
Same but operates on previous match
Skip this match, repeat at the next

blocks.title: Insert-mode dot records a transcript
blocks.text: When the recorded change includes an insert session, the editor stores the full transcript of typed characters, newlines, and editing keys ({key:Ctrl-W}, {key:Ctrl-U}, {key:Ctrl-T}) — but not arrow-key cursor moves. That exclusion is deliberate: arrows would make replay depend on positions in the original buffer that don't exist in the new one. Replay re-runs the transcript through the same insert-mode handlers.
